Now I have a clear picture of the current state:

1. The main cluster `es-cluster` has 3 nodes: `es-nodes-0`, `es-nodes-1`, `es-nodes-2` (StatefulSet `es-nodes` with 3 replicas)
2. The `app-data` index has 2 shards: primary on `es-nodes-1` and replica on `es-nodes-0`
3. There are also separate clusters: `es-alpha` (3 nodes) and `es-beta` (1 node) - these are separate clusters entirely
4. I need to add 2 new data nodes to `es-cluster` while keeping the existing StatefulSet at 3 replicas, using allocation attributes to control shard placement and ensure `app-data` shards migrate to the new nodes.

I'm weighing two approaches: either add an allocation attribute to the existing nodes (which requires a rolling restart and carries some risk), or create new nodes with a distinct attribute while leaving the originals unchanged. The second option avoids modifying the current setup but relies on the presence/absence of the attribute to differentiate nodes. I'll explore using index routing policies to direct shards to the new nodes based on their allocation attribute.

For the new StatefulSet, I need to ensure it can join the existing cluster by configuring the same TLS certificates, setting up a headless service for DNS discovery, pointing to the existing nodes for cluster discovery, and keeping it out of the initial master nodes list. Let me examine the current StatefulSet configuration more closely to understand the volume claims and other details I'll need to replicate. I also need to set up shard allocation routing on the `app-data` label to direct traffic to the new nodes. input.command:
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: es-data-config
  namespace: elasticsearch
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    node.name: ${NODE_NAME}
    node.roles: [data]
    node.attr.rack: new
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    http.port: 9200
    transport.port: 9300
    discovery.seed_hosts:
      - es-nodes-0.es-nodes.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
      - es-nodes-1.es-nodes.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
      - es-nodes-2.es-nodes.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-data
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: es-data
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 9200
    - name: transport
      port: 9300
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: es-data
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-data
  replicas: 2
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: es-data
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: es-data
    spec:
      securityContext:
        fsGroup: 1000
        runAsUser: 1000
      containers:
        - name: elasticsearch
          image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
          env:
            - name: NODE_NAME
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: metadata.name
            - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: elastic-password
                  key: password
            - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
              value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 9200
              name: http
            - containerPort: 9300
              name: transport
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - bash
                - -c
                - |
                  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
                    --cacert /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt \
                    -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                    https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | grep -qE "^(200)$"
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
            failureThreshold: 30
            timeoutSeconds: 10
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
            limits:
              memory: 2Gi
          volumeMounts:
            - name: es-data-config
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
              subPath: elasticsearch.yml
            - name: es-certs
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
              readOnly: true
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      volumes:
        - name: es-data-config
          configMap:
            name: es-data-config
        - name: es-certs
          secret:
            secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data
      spec:
        accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 5Gi
EOF

input.command:
# Route app-data shards to new nodes
kubectl exec -n elasticsearch es-nodes-0 -c elasticsearch -- curl -sk -u "elastic:elasticpass" \
  -X PUT "https://localhost:9200/app-data/_settings" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"index.routing.allocation.require.rack": "new"}' 2>/dev/null
echo ""
echo "---"
# Also check cluster health
kubectl exec -n elasticsearch es-nodes-0 -c elasticsearch -- curl -sk -u "elastic:elasticpass" \
  "https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health?pretty" 2>/dev/null
